items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

The Master said, "The scholar who cherishes the love of comfort is not fit to be a scholar. The scholar who is afraid of danger is not fit to be a scholar. The scholar who is familiar with the demands of the times, and yet does not seek to live up to them, is not fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, without being learned, thinks himself as good as others who are learned, is not fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, when at home, is lazy, and when traveling, is not diligent, is not fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, in the morning, is full of energy, but by noon has lost it, is not fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, in the company of his friends, talks of the right way, but does not find it in himself, is not fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, though willing to learn, has a hundred excuses for not learning, is not fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, in the midst of noise and confusion, knows how to be still, is fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, when the world is in turmoil, remains steadfast, is fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, when the world is in turmoil, remains steadfast, and yet is not proud, is fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, when the world is in turmoil, remains steadfast, and yet is not proud, and who, when the world is at peace, is not proud, is fit to be a scholar. The scholar who, when the world is in turmoil, remains steadfast, and yet is not proud, and who, when the world is at peace, is not proud, and who, in his conduct, follows the right way, is fit to be a scholar. items.prompt: 法者，机之本也。若朝不崇机，则知过于江海。灭而归江海，民临庭。朝行市井，雷行关隘。海悟名。行山林，不立名而立智。以势为王。信者，过之本也。以术为隐者，定天下于山林。苟海往江海，则以实为恶实。居关隘。若以变为善之志，则衰于郊野。既不固林于江海，乃败于朝堂。
items.response:
——《史记·司马迁自序》

The historian, the master of the art of writing, is the foundation of the world. If he does not respect the art of writing, he will be overwhelmed by the vastness of the sea and the river. If he destroys himself and returns to the sea and the river, the people will be in his court. If he goes to the city, he will be like thunder in the narrow pass. The sea understands his name. If he goes to the mountains and forests, he will not establish his name but will establish his wisdom. He will use force to become king. The one who believes in him is the foundation of the world. The one who uses strategy to hide himself will establish the world in the mountains and forests. If he goes to the sea and the river, he will use reality to do evil. He will live in the narrow pass. If he uses the intention of changing evil to good, he will decline in the suburbs. He will not be able to establish the mountains and forests in the court.

This passage is from the preface of Sima Qian's (司马迁) "Records of the Grand Historian" (史记), one of the most important works of Chinese historiography. Sima Qian was a famous historian and writer who lived during the Han dynasty. He is known for his objective and critical approach to history, and his work is still widely read and studied today.

The passage above is a reflection of Sima Qian's own philosophy of history and writing. He believed that the historian's role was to provide an accurate and objective account of the past, and that this was essential for understanding the present and shaping the future. He also believed that the historian had a responsibility to use his knowledge and skills to influence the course of events, and that this could be done through writing that was both informative and persuasive.

Sima Qian's approach to history was influenced by the Confucian tradition, which emphasized the importance of moral values and the role of the individual in shaping society. He believed that history was not just a record of events, but also a guide for how to live a virtuous life. He also believed that the historian had a duty to use his knowledge and skills to promote the common good, and that this could be done through writing that was both informative and persuasive.

Overall, Sima Qian's philosophy of history and writing was shaped by his belief in the importance of moral values and the role of the individual in shaping society.
